questions.prompt: An automotive engineering team is evaluating gigacasting for a new program. What questions tell them whether to commit?

choices.text: Does Tesla do it? If yes, copy them.

Volume per program (must be >200K/year)? Design stability (mid-cycle refreshes cost millions in new dies)? Capital availability (sustained capex)? Repair-cost externality acceptable (insurance, customer TCO)? Supply chain redundancy plan? Hybrid path viable for risk management? Below scale or with frequent design changes, stamping economics still win.

questions.explanation: Gigacasting commitment requires confidence on scale, design stability, capital, and risk tolerance. The decision must be made deliberately per program, not as a blanket strategy. Hybrid approaches (cast structural sub-assemblies, stamp the rest) lower risk for those who can't commit to full gigacasting.

questions.prompt: What's the structural risk gigacasting creates beyond per-vehicle economics?

choices.text: Supply chain concentration. Press suppliers (Idra dominant), alloy producers, die makers are all thin markets. A die delay = months of production delay. Alloy supply tightness slows everything. Press downtime = direct production loss with no easy redundancy. Stamping has mature, diversified supply chains; gigacasting doesn't. Combined with high fixed-cost capex, the risk profile is meaningfully different.

questions.explanation: Beyond per-unit economics, gigacasting's structural risk is supply chain concentration. Mature stamping supply chains diversify risk; thin casting supply chains amplify it. One bad supplier event can shut down a Gigafactory's body output for months.
